channel.title: NPR Topics: Education
channel.description: We've been to school. We know how education works. Right? In fact, many aspects of learning — in homes, at schools, at work and elsewhere — are evolving rapidly, along with our understanding of learning. Join us as we explore how learning happens.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: The Trump administration is investigating Smith College for admitting trans women
item.description: Smith College is one of the largest women's colleges in the country. This week, the Trump administration announced it is investigating the school's practice of admitting trans women.
item.content:encoded: Smith College is one of the largest women's colleges in the country. This week, the Trump administration announced it is investigating the school's practice of admitting trans women.
item.dc:creator: Nirvani Williams

item.title: More college applicants are opting to include SAT or ACT scores in their submissions
item.description: This application cycle, way more students reported test scores, up 10% compared with last year. That's despite many colleges having "test-optional" admissions policies.
item.content:encoded: This application cycle, way more students reported test scores, up 10% compared with last year. That's despite many colleges having "test-optional" admissions policies.
item.dc:creator: JD Allen

item.title: What actually happens when schools take away students' phones?
item.description: NPR's Ailsa Chang speaks with Stanford education economist Thomas Dee about the latest study on schools banning cellphones.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ailsa Chang speaks with Stanford education economist Thomas Dee about the latest study on schools banning cellphones.
item.dc:creator: Brianna Scott

item.title: A new association of 15 HBCUs aims to collectively boost their research status
item.description: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to Morgan State University president David K. Wilson about an association aimed at boosting the research status of Historically Black Colleges and Universities.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Ayesha Rascoe speaks to Morgan State University president David K. Wilson about an association aimed at boosting the research status of Historically Black Colleges and Universities.
item.dc:creator: Ayesha Rascoe

item.title: Several states — and the LA public schools — are setting limits on screen time
item.description: Four states have recently passed legislation to limit teaching and assessments via screens for students. So has the United States' second-largest school district.
item.content:encoded: Four states have recently passed legislation to limit teaching and assessments via screens for students. So has the United States' second-largest school district.
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Carrillo

item.title: Linda McMahon punches back at senators questioning Education Department cuts
item.description: In her first appearance on Capitol Hill this year, lawmakers questioned Education Secretary Linda McMahon about students' civil rights and cuts to federal education spending.
item.content:encoded:
In her first appearance on Capitol Hill this year, lawmakers questioned Education Secretary Linda McMahon about students' civil rights and cuts to federal education spending.

(Image credit: Aaron Schwartz)
item.dc:creator: Jonaki Mehta

item.title: As school choice expands in Iowa, one district is in a crisis from losing students
item.description: With school choice programs ascendant not just in Iowa but across the U.S., Cedar Rapids offers a preview of who wins and who loses when education meets the free market.
item.content:encoded: With school choice programs ascendant not just in Iowa but across the U.S., Cedar Rapids offers a preview of who wins and who loses when education meets the free market.
item.dc:creator: Cory Turner

item.title: Should schools get rid of homework? Some educators are saying yes
item.description: Some experts worry that less homework could be a problem for math achievement, at a time when test scores nationwide are already at a dismal low.
item.content:encoded:
Some experts worry that less homework could be a problem for math achievement, at a time when test scores nationwide are already at a dismal low.

(Image credit: Stanislaw Pytel)
item.dc:creator: Ariel Gilreath

item.title: Inflation is sucking the life out of teacher pay raises, report says
item.description: A new review of state education data shows teacher pay increases can't keep up with inflation and fewer students are enrolled in public schools.
item.content:encoded: A new review of state education data shows teacher pay increases can't keep up with inflation and fewer students are enrolled in public schools.
item.dc:creator: Cory Turner

item.title: Why emotional disturbance, a special ed category, is a double-edged sword for students
item.description: Every school has problem students, but some are labeled emotionally disturbed (ED) and taught separately from others.
item.content:encoded:
Every school has problem students, but some are labeled emotionally disturbed (ED) and taught separately from others.

(Image credit: Yasmin Yassin for NPR)
item.dc:creator: Laurie Stern
